papers.title: P. succinatutens: Succinate Scavenging & Pathogen Inhibition
papers.abstract: P. succinatutens scavenges luminal succinate through its obligate succinate-to-propionate metabolic pathway, depriving opportunistic pathogens (Enterobacteriaceae) of a critical carbon source while simultaneously lowering luminal pH via propionate production. The methylmalonyl-CoA pathway is fully validated in vitro and in murine mono-colonization models (Level 3). Core enzymes (ScpA/ScpB/ScpC) show 100% amino acid sequence identity between two Phascolarctobacterium type strains. In porcine models, this function reaches only Level 1 (co-occurrence network inference). Critical evidence gap (Level 4 void): porcine-derived strain isolation and mono-colonization in weaned piglets is entirely absent.
papers.modelSystem: In vitro + Murine mono-colonization (L3); Porcine co-occurrence network (L1)
papers.keyLimitation: No porcine-derived strain isolated; no mono-colonization in piglets performed. Translational bottleneck: murine L3 to porcine L4.
papers.forwardJustification: P. succinatutens succinate-to-propionate pathway fully characterized in vitro. Methylmalonyl-CoA pathway enzymes (ScpA/ScpB/ScpC) 100% conserved. Murine mono-colonization validates causal propionate production.
papers.reverseJustification: No evidence for host control of P. succinatutens abundance or activity. Reverse pathway entirely uncharacterized in any model species.
papers.summary: Benchmark case for the translational gap. P. succinatutens succinate-to-propionate pathway is mechanistically validated at L3 in mice but only inferred from co-occurrence networks at L1 in pigs. Represents the critical evidence void at the murine-to-porcine translational interface. B12-dependence of the methylmalonyl-CoA pathway creates a functional intersection between the probiotic and Vitamin B12 nodes.

papers.title: P. succinatutens: Intestinal Barrier Maintenance via Propionate
papers.abstract: P. succinatutens maintains intestinal barrier integrity through propionate via a dual-pathway mechanism: (1) membrane pathway: GPR43/GPR41 activation rerouting signals through beta-arrestin and cAMP branches to block NF-kappaB nuclear translocation; (2) intracellular pathway: HDAC inhibition relieving epigenetic repression of tight junction proteins (ZO-1, occludin). In murine models, the complete causal chain of mono-colonization to propionate production to TLR4/NF-kappaB inhibition to barrier maintenance is established (Level 3). Two Level 2 studies in pigs independently validated propionate-mediated TLR4/NF-kappaB suppression and tight junction protein upregulation. Key limitation: porcine evidence validates propionate's barrier-protective effect but does not demonstrate that P. succinatutens produces sufficient propionate in situ for this effect.
papers.modelSystem: Murine mono-colonization (L3); Porcine dietary fiber fermentation models (L2)
papers.keyLimitation: Strain-specific causal validation missing in pigs: propionate's barrier effect validated, but P. succinatutens' in situ propionate production capacity in porcine gut not demonstrated.
papers.summary: Best-validated probiotic function node. Dual-pathway propionate signaling mechanism fully characterized. Translational gap: strain-specific propionate production quantification in porcine gut lumen and epithelium needed to advance from L2 to L3 in pigs.

papers.title: P. succinatutens: Lipid Deposition Reduction via MYC-Arginine-FOXO3a Axis
papers.abstract: P. succinatutens reduces host lipid deposition through the propionate-mediated MYC-arginine-FOXO3a molecular axis: HDAC inhibition leads to MYC transcriptional attenuation, derepression of glutamine transporters (SLC1A5/SLC38A5) enhances de novo arginine biosynthesis, and arginine accumulation suppresses PI3K/Akt/FOXO3a signaling to reduce adipogenic gene expression. Murine FMT and mono-colonization experiments have fully validated this pathway (Level 3). In porcine models, UCP1 knock-in pigs show negative correlation between fecal SCFAs and adipogenic gene expression (Level 1 association). The pathway is not P. succinatutens-specific in the porcine context. Functional divergence from P. faecium (which operates via TLR2) constitutes genus-level metabolic specialization.
papers.modelSystem: Murine FMT + mono-colonization (L3); Porcine UCP1 knock-in correlation (L1)
papers.keyLimitation: Pathway validated in mice only; porcine evidence is SCFA-adipogenic gene correlation, not P. succinatutens-specific. Pathway conservation across commercial pig breeds is speculative.
papers.summary: Demonstrates genus-level functional divergence (P. succinatutens vs P. faecium). MYC-arginine-FOXO3a axis is a potential target for lipid reduction in livestock. Porcine validation remains entirely at association level.

papers.title: Butyrate/SCFAs: Compartmentalized Co-Metabolism Node (Priority P1)
papers.abstract: Butyrate is the most extensively validated node in the compartmentalized co-metabolism network. Forward pathway (microbial metabolites to host epigenetics): Level 2b in vivo validation in porcine models. Xiong et al. (2016) landmark study demonstrated butyrate upregulates endogenous host defense peptides via HDAC inhibition in weaned piglets. Porcine ileal organoid experiments confirmed HDAC inhibition and barrier-enhancing effects. Tibetan pig studies showed butyrate promotes macrophage H4K31 crotonylation via mTORC1-HDAC3/8. Reverse pathway (host epigenetics to microbiota): only Level 1. Host gene regulatory networks (PRDM15, STAT1) and germ-free pig transcriptome data provide hypothesis-generating clues. Coupling: Level 0. Forward and reverse effects never simultaneously observed in a single experimental system. Measurement Depth: Level 0. All studies measured only a single compartment (luminal) at a single timepoint. The three-order-of-magnitude concentration difference between luminal (mM) and intracellular (muM) butyrate has not been incorporated into experimental design.
papers.modelSystem: Porcine in vivo (weaned piglets, Tibetan pigs) + ileal organoids
papers.keyLimitation: Forward pathway at L2b but reverse pathway at L1 and coupling at L0. The fundamental butyrate concentration gradient (lumen mM vs epithelium muM) has never been incorporated into experimental design. No study has simultaneously measured forward and reverse effects.
papers.summary: Most-validated co-metabolism node. Forward pathway well-characterized at L2b in pigs (HDAC inhibition, host defense peptides, crotonylation). But reverse pathway, coupling verification, and multi-compartment measurement all at L0-L1. Represents the core evidence asymmetry in the co-metabolism framework.

papers.title: Bile Acids: Host-Microbiome Signaling Node (Priority P2)
papers.abstract: Bile acids are a porcine-validated core node. Forward pathway: Level 2b. Hyodeoxycholic acid (HDCA), the dominant porcine bile acid, enhances intestinal barrier function via FXR-PI3K/AKT pathway in weaned piglets, increasing Lactobacillus abundance from 5.28% to 37.97%. Neonatal piglets show bile acid-mediated epithelial proliferation and barrier maturation through TGR5 activation and FXR downregulation. Substantial species differences in bile acid pool composition (pigs: HDCA-dominant; mice: CA/DCA-dominant) limit murine evidence extrapolation. Reverse pathway: only Level 1. Whether host bile acid signaling alterations feed back to regulate microbial community composition has not been causally validated. Coupling: Level 0. FXR/TGR5 activation and microbial restructuring never simultaneously tracked. Measurement Depth: Level 0. Main evidence gap: concentration gradient data for bile acids at the lumen-epithelium interface are absent.
papers.keyLimitation: Species-specific bile acid composition (HDCA-dominant in pigs) renders murine data non-directly translatable. Reverse pathway causal validation void is the critical bottleneck for understanding bile acid-microbiota feedback circuits.
papers.summary: Porcine-validated forward pathway with species-specific bile acid (HDCA). Lactobacillus expansion from 5.3% to 38.0% is the strongest microbial community shift documented in any node. Reverse pathway (host-to-microbiome bile acid signaling) is the critical evidence gap. FXR-dependent antimicrobial peptide pathway recently identified as potential reverse mechanism.

papers.title: Tryptophan Metabolites: AhR Signaling Node (Priority P2)
papers.abstract: Tryptophan metabolites are partially porcine-validated. Forward pathway: Level 2a (preliminary in vivo). Bacteroides thetaiotaomicron alleviates weaning diarrhea in piglets via tryptophan metabolism-AhR-Nrf2 signaling. Gut microbiota-mediated tryptophan metabolism suppresses pyroptosis via AhR signaling to maintain intestinal epithelial homeostasis in piglets. Dietary tryptophan supplementation modulates gut microbial composition and activates AhR signaling. Validations are based on specific bacterial strains or dietary interventions, not direct causal validation of tryptophan metabolites per se. Reverse pathway: Level 0. Tryptophan metabolite reverse regulatory role completely unexplored. Coupling: Level 0. Measurement Depth: Level 0. Core evidence gap: concentration gradients of tryptophan metabolites (indole, kynurenine) across lumen-epithelium-microenvironment compartments never simultaneously measured. Temporal asynchrony between AhR activation and microbial community shifts uncharacterized.
papers.keyLimitation: Validations are strain-specific (B. thetaiotaomicron) or dietary, not direct metabolite causal validation. AhR activation and pyroptosis suppression not linked causally in porcine gut in vivo. Reverse pathway entirely unexplored.
papers.summary: AhR signaling is the central mechanism linking tryptophan metabolites to intestinal homeostasis. Strain-specific (B. thetaiotaomicron) and dietary intervention evidence at L2a. Direct metabolite causal validation, reverse pathway, and multi-compartment measurement all at L0.

papers.title: Polyamines: Cross-Species Extrapolation Node (Priority P3)
papers.abstract: Polyamines are a cross-species extrapolated predicted node with no direct validation evidence at any level in porcine models. Forward pathway: only Level 1. All existing evidence derives from murine models and in vitro studies: polyamines regulate histone methylation and acetylation by promoting nucleosome condensation; polyamines and gut microbiota form bidirectional interactions maintaining intestinal homeostasis (including microbial growth and biofilm formation). None of these mechanisms have been experimentally verified in the porcine gut. Reverse pathway: Level 0. Coupling: Level 0. Measurement Depth: Level 0. Core evidence gaps: baseline concentration and spatial distribution data for polyamines in the porcine gut are entirely absent; polyamine biosynthetic capacity of porcine-derived strains uncharacterized; polyamine transporter expression patterns in porcine intestinal epithelium unknown.
papers.modelSystem: Murine models and in vitro only
papers.keyLimitation: No porcine data at any level. Baseline polyamine concentrations in porcine gut unknown. Porcine-derived polyamine-producing strains not isolated. Entirely dependent on cross-species extrapolation from murine evidence.
papers.summary: Weakest evidence base among all five nodes in porcine context. All evidence is murine-derived. Inclusion based on established mammalian host-microbe co-metabolic functions. Porcine functional relevance requires de novo establishment. First essential step: baseline polyamine concentration measurement in porcine gut compartments.

papers.title: Vitamin B12: One-Carbon Metabolism Node (Priority P3)
papers.abstract: Vitamin B12 has the weakest evidence base among all five nodes. Forward pathway: only Level 1 (association inference). B12 is a key cofactor in host one-carbon metabolism, participating in S-adenosylmethionine (SAM) synthesis to provide methyl donors for DNA methylation. This theoretical link is elaborated in reviews but direct causal validation between B12 concentrations and DNA methylation marks is absent in porcine models. In P. succinatutens' succinate-to-propionate pathway, B12 is the essential coenzyme for methylmalonyl-CoA mutase. B12 availability directly impacts propionate production efficiency, representing the functional intersection with Project 1. Reverse pathway: Level 0. Coupling: Level 0. Measurement Depth: Level 0. Core evidence gaps: B12 concentration distribution across porcine gut compartments unknown; B12-propionate metabolic coupling never validated in situ in porcine gut; causal effect of B12 on porcine intestinal epithelial DNA methylation untested.
papers.modelSystem: Theoretical/review-level only; no direct experimental validation
papers.keyLimitation: Weakest of all five nodes. Entirely at theoretical/review level. No experimental data on B12-DNA methylation link in porcine gut. B12-propionate coupling in situ completely untested. P. succinatutens B12 dependence is the only functional anchor in the co-metabolism network.
papers.summary: Weakest evidence node but critical for understanding one-carbon metabolism integration with microbial SCFA production. Functional intersection with P. succinatutens (methylmalonyl-CoA mutase requires B12) provides the mechanistic rationale for inclusion. Entirely a hypothesis awaiting experimental testing in porcine models.

papers.title: Propionate: Succinate-to-Propionate Pathway Node (Priority P1)
papers.abstract: Propionate is a major microbial SCFA in the porcine gut, produced via the succinate pathway (dominant in Bacteroidetes and Phascolarctobacterium) and the acrylate pathway (dominant in Firmicutes). Forward pathway (microbial propionate → host): Level 2b in pigs. Propionate activates GPR41/GPR43 in porcine intestinal epithelium, suppresses HDAC activity, and serves as a gluconeogenic substrate in the porcine liver. The P. succinatutens methylmalonyl-CoA pathway directly couples succinate consumption to propionate production, representing a B12-dependent metabolic intersection. Reverse pathway (host → microbial propionate): Level 1. Dietary fiber composition and host bile acid secretion patterns influence propionate-producing community structure, but direct host genetic control over propionate output is uncharacterized. Key evidence gap: propionate concentration gradients across the porcine gut epithelium have never been systematically mapped. Bidirectional coupling remains at Level 0.
papers.modelSystem: Porcine dietary intervention + in vitro bacterial cultures
papers.keyLimitation: Propionate concentration gradients across gut compartments unmapped. P. succinatutens in situ propionate production rate in porcine gut unknown. Reverse pathway entirely at association level. B12-propionate coupling never measured in vivo.
papers.summary: Propionate is the second-most abundant SCFA in the porcine hindgut and a core output of the succinate-to-propionate pathway. Wraps P. succinatutens metabolism (succinate→propionate), B12 cofactor requirement (methylmalonyl-CoA mutase), and host GPR41/43 signaling into a single integrated node. Forward pathway partially validated at L2b in pigs; reverse pathway and coupling entirely unexplored.
papers.forwardJustification: Propionate-GPR41/43 signaling validated in porcine epithelium. HDAC inhibition and gluconeogenic roles characterized. P. succinatutens pathway fully mapped in vitro.
papers.reverseJustification: No causal evidence for host control of propionate-producing community in pigs. Dietary fiber → propionate association established but no host genetic or immune mechanism characterized.

papers.title: Acetate: Cross-Feeding & Systemic Signaling Node (Priority P2)
papers.abstract: Acetate is the most abundant SCFA in the porcine gut (typically 50-60% of total SCFAs) and the primary substrate for butyrate-producing bacteria via cross-feeding. Forward pathway (microbial acetate → host): Level 2a in pigs. Acetate crosses the gut epithelium via MCT1/SMCT1 transporters, enters the portal circulation, and reaches peripheral tissues at high concentrations (100-200 muM in human portal blood; porcine data limited). Acetate activates GPR43 on enteroendocrine L-cells, stimulates GLP-1/PYY secretion, and serves as a lipogenic substrate and epigenetic modifier (acetyl-CoA for histone acetylation). Reverse pathway (host → acetate producers): Level 1. Host diet composition and intestinal transit time influence acetogenic community structure, but host-driven feedback mechanisms are unexplored. Key evidence gap: the gut microbial acetate economy in pigs — production rates, cross-feeding fluxes, and epithelial transport kinetics — lacks systematic quantification.
papers.modelSystem: Porcine dietary fiber models (association level); Murine mono-colonization (causal)
papers.keyLimitation: Acetate cross-feeding network structure in porcine gut unknown. Portal acetate concentration data absent for pigs. Acetate contribution to host histone acetylation in porcine intestinal epithelium never quantified. Reverse pathway entirely at theoretical level.
papers.summary: Acetate is quantitatively dominant but mechanistically understudied in pigs. Its role as the primary cross-feeding substrate for butyrate producers makes it a critical upstream node in the SCFA network. Porcine-specific portal and peripheral acetate kinetics are a major data gap. Histone acetylation link offers a direct epigenetic readout for microbial acetate activity.
papers.forwardJustification: GPR43 activation and GLP-1/PYY secretion by acetate validated in murine models. Acetate-to-butyrate cross-feeding well-characterized in vitro. Porcine evidence limited to dietary fiber → acetate correlations.
papers.reverseJustification: No evidence for host-directed control of acetogenic community composition. Host diet as driver established; host immune/genetic feedback unexplored.

papers.title: Folate/B9: One-Carbon Metabolism & Methyl Donor Node (Priority P2)
papers.abstract: Folate (vitamin B9) is a key microbial product in the gut that participates in host one-carbon metabolism alongside B12. Gut bacteria (especially Bifidobacterium and Lactobacillus species) are net folate producers, contributing 10-30% of host folate requirements. Forward pathway (microbial folate → host): Level 1 in pigs. Microbial folate is absorbed in the colon via reduced folate carrier (RFC) and proton-coupled folate transporter (PCFT), contributing to host methyl donor pools (SAM cycle) and nucleotide synthesis. Bifidobacterium adolescentis folate production has been demonstrated in vitro and in murine models, but porcine in vivo evidence is absent. Reverse pathway (host → microbial folate): Level 1. Host dietary folate intake and the host folate absorption efficiency set the competitive landscape for microbial folate producers vs. auxotrophs. In pigs, dietary folic acid supplementation alters gut microbial composition but the mechanism (direct vs. indirect via host metabolism) is unclear. Coupling: Level 0. Measurement Depth: Level 0. Key gap: gut luminal folate concentrations and microbial folate production rates in pigs are completely unknown.
papers.modelSystem: Murine Bifidobacterium mono-association (L2a); Porcine dietary folic acid (L1 association)
papers.keyLimitation: Porcine gut luminal folate concentrations unknown. Microbial folate production rates in pig gut never measured. Bifidobacterium/Lactobacillus folate contribution to host methyl donor pool unquantified in any large animal model. B12-folate metabolic intersection in porcine gut unexplored.
papers.summary: Folate represents the microbial contribution to host one-carbon metabolism at the methyl donor level. Bifidobacterium and Lactobacillus are the primary porcine-relevant folate producers. The B12-folate intersection in one-carbon metabolism is mechanistically established in human/murine systems but untested in pigs. Luminal concentration and production rate data are the prerequisite measurements.
papers.forwardJustification: Microbial folate production demonstrated in Bifidobacterium and Lactobacillus in vitro. Colonic folate absorption via RFC/PCFT characterized in rodents. Porcine evidence: dietary folic acid changes gut microbiota composition at association level only.
papers.reverseJustification: Host dietary folate status influences competitive dynamics between folate producers and auxotrophs. Porcine dietary folic acid → microbiota shift observed but mechanistic pathway (direct microbial vs. host-mediated) not resolved.

papers.title: Vitamin A/Retinoic Acid: Immune-Microbiome Signaling Node (Priority P2)
papers.abstract: Vitamin A and its active metabolite retinoic acid (RA) sit at the host-microbiome interface, regulating both host immunity and gut microbial ecology. Forward pathway (microbe-influenced RA → host): Level 2a in pigs. Gut bacteria influence host vitamin A metabolism through: (1) microbial production of retinaldehyde dehydrogenase (RALDH) cofactors that modulate host RA synthesis; (2) microbial SCFAs promoting dendritic cell RALDH expression and RA production. Porcine weaning stress decreases intestinal RALDH activity, correlating with microbial community disruption. Reverse pathway (host RA → microbiome): Level 2b in pigs. Retinoic acid directly regulates the gut microbiome through: (1) RA-induced epithelial IL-22/STAT3 signaling altering AMP secretion profiles; (2) RA-RAR signaling in B cells promoting IgA class switching, which shapes microbial composition; (3) RA modulation of Treg/Th17 balance influencing immune tolerance to commensals. This reverse pathway is better characterized than most nodes because the RA-RAR axis is well-studied. Coupling: Level 0 — RA effects on host immunity and microbial composition never tracked simultaneously in pigs. Measurement Depth: Level 0.
papers.modelSystem: Porcine weaning stress models (L2a); Murine RAR knockout + gnotobiotic (L3)
papers.keyLimitation: Simultaneous measurement of RA-dependent host immune activation AND microbial community shifts not performed in pigs. Porcine intestinal RA concentration and RALDH activity spatial mapping absent. The RA-RAR-IgA axis relevance to porcine gut commensal control unquantified.
papers.summary: Vitamin A/retinoic acid is the best-characterized REVERSE pathway node. The RA-RAR-IgA axis provides a mechanistically detailed host-to-microbiome signaling route. The forward pathway (microbes → host RA metabolism) is less characterized, creating an unusual reverse-dominant evidence asymmetry. Key AhR-RA crosstalk at the tryptophan-vitamin A metabolic intersection is unexplored in pigs.
papers.forwardJustification: Porcine weaning stress reduces intestinal RALDH activity (enzyme-level evidence). Microbial SCFA promotion of DC RALDH expression established in mice. Causal microbial → RA → host immune effect not demonstrated in pigs.
papers.reverseJustification: RA-RAR signaling in B cell IgA class switching well-characterized (molecular pathway). RA-IL-22-STAT3-AMP axis established. Porcine dietary vitamin A alters gut microbiota composition. Strongest reverse pathway evidence among all non-Bile Acid nodes at L2b.

papers.title: P. succinatutens: Porcine Gut Strain Isolation & Characterization Status
papers.abstract: Phascolarctobacterium succinatutens is an obligate succinate-consuming, propionate-producing bacterium in the family Acidaminococcaceae. It occupies a keystone metabolic niche in the porcine gut: its succinate-to-propionate pathway prevents luminal succinate accumulation (which would otherwise fuel Enterobacteriaceae blooms) while producing propionate for host signaling. Current isolation status: YIT 12066 and YIT 12067 were isolated from human feces (Yakult Central Institute, 2012). NO porcine-derived strain has been isolated to date. 16S rRNA gene surveys place Phascolarctobacterium at 1-5% relative abundance in healthy pig feces across diverse breeds (Yorkshire, Landrace, Duroc, Meishan). Metagenomic binning has recovered near-complete P. succinatutens genomes from pig fecal metagenomes. Core metabolic pathway (methylmalonyl-CoA) shows 95-100% amino acid sequence identity between human and porcine metagenome-assembled genomes. Key bottleneck: without a porcine-derived isolate, mono-colonization experiments in piglets cannot be performed, blocking L3→L4 translational advance.
papers.modelSystem: Porcine 16S/metagenomic surveys (L1); Human isolate in vitro (L2); Murine cross-colonization (L3)
papers.keyLimitation: No porcine-derived P. succinatutens isolate exists — the single most important bottleneck for advancing the translational pipeline from murine L3 to porcine L4. Without a pig-derived strain, causal validation in piglets is impossible.
papers.summary: P. succinatutens is the highest-priority strain for porcine isolation. Its succinate-to-propionate pathway is the mechanistic center of the co-metabolism framework. The human isolate (YIT 12066/12067) provides a genetic template, but porcine strain isolation is the prerequisite for advancing the entire Project 1 translational pipeline from L3 to L4. Core metabolic pathway is conserved but host-specific colonization factors remain unknown.
papers.forwardJustification: Succinate-to-propionate pathway fully characterized in human isolates. Methylmalonyl-CoA pathway enzymes (ScpA/ScpB/ScpC) 95-100% conserved in porcine MAGs. In vitro propionate production demonstrated. Host signaling effects inferred from propionate node.
papers.reverseJustification: Host dietary and immunological factors influencing P. succinatutens abundance in pig gut completely uncharacterized. Porcine intestinal factors driving niche specialization unknown. Dietary succinate availability is the only characterized host input.

papers.title: Lactobacillus: Porcine Gut Probiotic Ecology Node (Priority P1)
papers.abstract: Lactobacillus species (including Lactiplantibacillus, Limosilactobacillus, and Ligilactobacillus per reclassified taxonomy) are dominant small intestinal colonizers in pigs, reaching 10^7-10^9 CFU/g in the duodenum and jejunum. They are the most extensively studied porcine gut bacteria, with >200 published porcine feeding trials. Forward pathway (Lactobacillus → host): Level 2b in pigs. Multiple porcine trials demonstrate Lactobacillus-mediated pathogen inhibition (E. coli K88, Salmonella Typhimurium), intestinal barrier enhancement (ZO-1, occludin upregulation), and immune modulation (TLR2/TLR4 signaling, IgA promotion). However, strain-level specificity is high — effects observed with one strain (e.g., L. plantarum JDFM LP11) do not generalize. Reverse pathway (host → Lactobacillus): Level 2a in pigs. Host dietary fiber composition, weaning stress, and intestinal mucin glycosylation patterns all influence Lactobacillus community composition. Host antimicrobial peptide secretion (RegIII-gamma) selectively suppresses Lactobacillus in a porcine weaning model. Key gap: strain-level resolution is required — genus-level 16S data obscures functional divergence between species/strains.
papers.modelSystem: Porcine in vivo (weaned piglets, >200 trials) + porcine intestinal organoids
papers.keyLimitation: Genus-level data dominate; strain-level functional divergence is critical but under-characterized. Reclassified taxonomy (Lactobacillus → 25 genera, 2020) not reflected in most porcine literature. Strain-specific colonization factors and persistence in porcine gut poorly understood.
papers.summary: Lactobacillus is the most extensively studied porcine gut bacterium but suffers from nomenclatural fragmentation and inadequate strain-level resolution. Forward pathway evidence at L2b (barrier, immune, pathogen inhibition), reverse pathway at L2a (host AMPs and weaning stress). Represents the best-characterized bidirectional system among all strain nodes but lacks coupling (never measured simultaneously in same animals).
papers.forwardJustification: Extensive porcine trial literature: barrier enhancement (ZO-1/occludin), pathogen inhibition (E. coli, Salmonella), immune modulation (TLR2/4, IgA). Strain specificity high — effects not generalizable across Lactobacillus species.
papers.reverseJustification: Porcine weaning stress alters Lactobacillus community structure. Host RegIII-gamma AMP selectively suppresses Lactobacillus in piglets. Dietary fiber → Lactobacillus bloom established. Host mucin glycosylation influence demonstrated at L2a.

papers.title: Prevotella: Porcine Hindgut Dominance & Succinate Node (Priority P2)
papers.abstract: Prevotella is the dominant Gram-negative genus in the porcine hindgut, often representing 15-30% of the fecal metagenome — substantially higher than in humans. Prevotella species are major succinate producers in the pig colon, making them the primary upstream metabolic partner for succinate-consuming bacteria (P. succinatutens). Forward pathway (Prevotella → host): Level 2a in pigs. Prevotella-dominated enterotypes are associated with higher luminal SCFA concentrations and improved feed efficiency in pigs. Prevotella copri produces succinate as a fermentation end-product, which is then cross-fed to propionate producers. Specific Prevotella species encode carbohydrate-active enzymes (CAZymes) targeting porcine dietary fibers (arabinoxylan, pectin). Reverse pathway (host → Prevotella): Level 2a. Host dietary carbohydrate composition is the dominant driver of Prevotella abundance. Porcine host genotype (MUC4, FUT1 polymorphisms) influences Prevotella colonization. Key gap: Prevotella-to-P. succinatutens succinate cross-feeding has never been directly measured in the porcine gut in situ. The succinate intermediate pool size and flux rate are unknown.
papers.keyLimitation: Prevotella-to-Phascolarctobacterium succinate cross-feeding never measured in situ in porcine gut. Succinate intermediate pool size unknown. Prevotella species-level functional diversity in pigs poorly characterized — genus-level data mask substantial metabolic heterogeneity.
papers.summary: Prevotella is the dominant succinate producer and primary metabolic partner for P. succinatutens in the porcine hindgut. The Prevotella→succinate→Phascolarctobacterium→propionate cascade is the central metabolite flow in the co-metabolism framework. Host dietary fiber composition is the dominant top-down driver of Prevotella abundance. Porcine-specific Prevotella CAZyme repertoire and succinate production kinetics are key unknowns.
papers.forwardJustification: Prevotella-dominated enterotype → higher SCFA and improved feed efficiency in multiple porcine cohorts. P. copri succinate production characterized in vitro. Porcine Prevotella CAZyme diversity documented at metagenomic level. Direct succinate flux to propionate producers not measured.
papers.reverseJustification: Host dietary carbohydrate composition is the dominant driver — well-established in multiple porcine dietary intervention trials. Host genotype (MUC4, FUT1) influence documented. Weaning transition shifts Prevotella abundance, driven by dietary change.

papers.title: Aryl Hydrocarbon Receptor Signaling in Aging: A Key Modulator of Brain Homeostasis.
papers.abstract: Aging results from the gradual accumulation of molecular and cellular damage, and it is a risk factor for neurodegenerative disease. The aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR) is increasingly recognized as a key in the regulatory molecular mechanisms that drive aging. AhR is a cytoplasmic receptor activated by diverse endogenous and exogenous ligands. It modulates cell proliferation and differentiation in development and the adult brain. Besides, AhR participates in multiple physiological mechanisms th

papers.title: Advances in schizophrenia research and treatment: exploring neurotransmitter imbalances, genetics, and innovative therapies.
papers.abstract: Schizophrenia is a severe mental disorder arising from genetic vulnerability, impaired neurodevelopment, neurobiological dysregulation, and environmental factors. Its pathophysiology involves disturbances in dopamine, glutamate, and serotonin systems, as well as neuroinflammation, oxidative stress, gut-brain axis dysfunction, and altered energy metabolism. Current dopaminergic pharmacotherapy is often complicated by insufficient efficacy, adverse effects, and patient non-compliance. This review

papers.title: Cognition in the time of dysbiosis: Sleep disruption and the gut-brain dialogue.
papers.abstract: The gut microbiota plays an important role in the regulation of physiological and behavioral functions, including cognition. Growing evidence suggests that alterations in microbial composition can disrupt memory processes, in part through bidirectional communication along the gut-brain axis, although additional mechanisms cannot be excluded. Both sleep deprivation and sleep fragmentation are known to impair memory consolidation. In this review, we examine how these sleep disturbances influence t

papers.title: Gut-pancreas-metabolism axis: emerging anti-diabetic roles of gut-derived bioactive molecules.
papers.abstract: The rising global burden of diabetes mellitus necessitates exploration of mechanisms beyond classical pancreatic dysfunction. The gut-pancreas-metabolism axis has emerged as a central regulatory network linking gut microbiota, enteroendocrine signaling, immune modulation, and pancreatic function in glucose homeostasis. Gut-derived bioactive metabolites, including short-chain fatty acids, bile acid derivatives, indole compounds, lipopolysaccharide fragments, and microbial peptides, significantly

papers.title: Astilbin ameliorates hyperuricemia-associated renal injury in geese with gut-linked kynurenic acid immunometabolic remodeling.
papers.abstract: Hyperuricemia (HUA) is increasingly recognized as a systemic inflammatory-metabolic disorder that contributes to immune-associated renal injury. Kynurenic acid (KYNA), a tryptophan-derived metabolite with reported immunomodulatory properties, has emerged as a potential mediator linking gut metabolism and distal organ inflammation. In this study, we investigated the protective effects of astilbin (ASB) against HUA and hyperuricemia-associated renal injury using an adenine-induced goose model, whi

papers.title: A Comprehensive Review on the Microbial Signatures and Metabolic Mechanisms Underlying the Gut-Alzheimer's Disease Axis.
papers.abstract: Alzheimer's disease (AD) is the most common form of dementia, driven by complex interactions among aging-related biological changes, neuronal degeneration, mitochondrial dysfunction, and environmental factors. Despite extensive research, effective disease-modifying therapies remain unavailable. Increasing evidence highlights the gut-brain axis as an important contributor to AD pathogenesis, particularly through amyloid-producing gut microbes that promote immune activation, neuroinflammation, and

papers.title: 3-Hydroxypropionic acid converts inflammatory macrophage glycolysis into mitochondrial oxidation through GAPDH carboxyethylation.
papers.abstract: Macrophages dynamically reprogram their metabolic states in response to environmental stimuli, thereby exerting distinct immune functions. In our preliminary study, a gut microbiota-derived metabolite, 3-hydroxypropionic acid (3-HPA), is increased in chronic inflammation and generates cysteine carboxyethylated neoantigens. However, the role of such metabolite-induced modification in regulating the function of macrophages remains obscure. Here, we show that 3-HPA alleviates inflammation in a mous

papers.title: Gut microbiota-derived succinate links proteostasis collapse to α-synuclein pathology and aging.
papers.abstract: The gut microbiome profoundly influences brain health, yet the specific microbial metabolites and mechanisms contributing to Parkinson's disease pathology remain poorly defined. Using the Caenorhabditis elegans model expressing human α-synuclein, we systematically tested key microbial fermentation products and identified succinate as a potent driver of pathology. Succinate exposure markedly increased α-synuclein aggregation, disrupted proteostasis, and compromised mitochondrial function - manife

papers.title: Bacteria-derived glutarate mitigates Alzheimer's disease model pathology through autophagy-lysosomal pathway.
papers.abstract: Probiotics exert neuroprotective effects against Alzheimer's disease (AD) by modulating gut-brain axis pathways, though disease-modifying therapies remain unavailable. Our study revealed that Escherichia coli (E. coli) strain HB101 ameliorated AD-related phenotypes in Caenorhabditis elegans‌ (C. elegans) models, including learning deficits, neurodegeneration, and paralysis. papers.title: Postbiotic Lactobacillus reuteri LRE02 Enriched with Sodium Selenite Demonstrates Antidepressant- and Anxiolytic-Like Effects in Mice.
papers.abstract: Postbiotics, including inactivated microorganisms and their bioactive components, have emerged as innovative candidates for microbiota-targeted therapies. Among them, Lactobacillus reuteri-derived postbiotics represent a promising and underexplored strategy for modulating host physiology without the risks associated with live bacteria. Selenium enrichment may further enhance these benefits by strengthening antioxidant defenses and regulating inflammation, two central mechanisms to microbiota-gut

papers.title: In Vitro Evaluation of Candidate Probiotic Bacteria Against Enterotoxigenic Escherichia coli (ETEC) Isolated from Post-Weaning Diarrhea in Piglets.
papers.abstract: Probiotics offer a promising alternative to antibiotics in animal husbandry. This study evaluated the inhibitory potential of live cells and cell-free supernatants (CFS) from 31 putative probiotic strains against two multidrug-resistant (MDR) enterotoxigenic Escherichia coli (ETEC) strains. Methods included antibiogram profiling, zone of inhibition assays, hemolytic activity, and auto-aggregation tests. Results highlighted strain-specific antimicrobial heterogeneity. papers.title: Mental health in early pregnancy: Interplay of objectively measured lifestyles, gut microbiota, and metabolomics.
papers.abstract: Prenatal anxiety symptoms (AS) and depression symptoms (DS) in early pregnancy substantially impact maternal-infant health, but their pathophysiological mechanisms remain unclear. We conducted a multimodal assessment of 161 early-pregnant women. DS exhibited significantly longer standing/sitting durations and shorter nocturnal sleep and nap times (p < 0.05). AS was associated with lower fiber intake (p = 0.018). AS and DS had more complex microbial co-occurrence networks with more extensive meta

papers.title: A Risk Score for Polycystic Ovary Syndrome Based on Meta-Analysis and Machine Learning of Gut Microbiota Signatures.
papers.abstract: Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS) is a prevalent endocrine and metabolic disorder among reproductive-age women, in which emerging evidence suggests a substantial role played by the gut microbiota. To comprehensively evaluate gut microbiota alterations in PCOS and identify microbial biomarkers through integrated analysis, a systematic search of PubMed, Web of Science, and Embase was conducted for studies employing 16S rRNA gene sequencing of fecal samples from PCOS cohorts. papers.title: Effects of Clostridium butyricum on the growth performance, digestion, immunity, and gut microbiota of Rana catesbiana.
papers.abstract: This study aimed to evaluated the comprehensive effects of dietary Clostridium butyricumon the growth performance, digestion, immunity, and gut microbiota of Rana catesbiana larvae.

papers.title: Metabolism of β-mannans by representative, understudied Bacillota species from the human colon.
papers.abstract: The macronutrients in our diets including non-starch polysaccharides such as β-mannans, found in plant cell walls, can impact on human gut health. There is however a paucity of data regarding the ability of gut bacteria, in particular those belonging to the Bacillota (previously Firmicutes) phylum, to depolymerise and ferment β-mannans. In this study, we tested a total of 12 strains, including nine Bacillota, for their ability to metabolise and cross-feed on β-mannans. Three of the six butyrate-

papers.title: Gut Microbiota-Generated Trimethylamine N-Oxide from Dietary Choline and Glucose Homeostasis in Humans.
papers.abstract: The causal relationship between gut microbiota-generated trimethylamine N-oxide (TMAO) and type 2 diabetes remains unclear. We tested the hypothesis that gut microbiota-generated increases in plasma TMAO concentrations will impair insulin sensitivity and glucose tolerance in healthy, sedentary humans. To address this, we performed two studies utilizing a randomized double-blind, placebo-controlled, crossover design. Eligible participants (age of 35 ± 16 years for the acute study and 46 ± 14 year
papers.journal: American journal of physiology. Endocrinology and metabolism

papers.title: Retinoic acid-driven expansion of CD16hiCD177+ neutrophils mediates steroid-resistant GI-GVHD.
papers.abstract: Steroid-resistant gastrointestinal graft-versus-host disease (SR-GI-GVHD) is a lethal complication of allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation with no clear therapeutic target. The role of neutrophil heterogeneity in its pathogenesis remains poorly defined. Through single-cell RNA sequencing of patient blood, we identified a specific neutrophil subset (CD16hiCD177⁺) that is markedly expanded in SR-GI-GVHD. This subset utilizes CD177 to bind endothelial CD31, facilitating transmigration

papers.title: Betulinic Acid Enema Alleviates Colitis by Targeting Vitamin D Receptor to Protect the Intestinal Mucosal Barrier and Modulate Mucosa-Associated Microbiota.
papers.abstract: Ulcerative colitis (UC) is a chronic inflammatory disease of the colon with limited effectiveness and safety of current drug therapies. Betulinic acid (BA) is a naturally occurring pentacyclic triterpenoid present in traditional Chinese medicines used clinically as retention enemas for UC, but its direct effects and mechanisms remain unclear. This study evaluated the therapeutic impact of BA enema in dextran sulfate sodium (DSS)-induced colitis by monitoring disease activity index (DAI), colon l

papers.title: A causal inference framework to bridge association and mechanism in the gut-brain axis.
papers.abstract: The gut-brain axis represents a major paradigm shift in how we evaluate diseases in neuroscience, with microbial dysbiosis affecting many neurological and psychiatric disorders. However, the clinical translation of these findings into effective therapies is currently stalled at a methodological impasse. This Causality Conundrum arises due to the fact that current models fail to resolve the bidirectional noise and cyclic feedback loops inherent in the gut-brain axis. These insufficiencies have ma

papers.title: Compound probiotics alleviate aflatoxin B1-induced reproductive toxicity in mice by remodeling gut microbiota and testicular metabolome via NRF2 pathway action.
papers.abstract: This study aimed to investigate the protective effects of compound probiotics (CP) comprising Saccharomyces cerevisiae, Lactobacillus casei, and Bacillus subtilis against aflatoxin B₁ (AFB₁) -induced reproductive toxicity in mice. Kunming mice (n = 60; male) were randomly assigned to six groups and treated via gavage for 28 d with control, AFB₁ alone (150 μg/kg), medium-dose CP (MCP) alone, or AFB₁ combined with low-, medium-, or high-dose CP. Notably, AFB₁ exposure significantly increased the i

papers.title: Traditional fermented camel milk from Xinjiang, China, used as a starter culture exhibits stable fermentation characteristics and anti-colitis effects.
papers.abstract: In this study, five traditional fermented camel milks obtained from five different pasturing areas in Xinjiang, China, were evaluated for their fermentation stability, microbiota composition, and amino acid and peptide profiles. The results demonstrated that traditional fermented camel milk from Xinjiang, when used as a starter culture, maintains stable pH, titratable acidity and viable bacterial counts during continuous camel milk fermentation. papers.title: A phase-variable capsule facilitates Akkermansia muciniphila colonization of the intestinal mucus layer.
papers.abstract: Akkermansia muciniphila is a mucophilic commensal bacterium that significantly impacts metabolic and immune homeostasis. However, the bacterial factors involved in colonization of the gastrointestinal tract are not well understood. Here, we clarify the role of capsular polysaccharides (CPS) in Akkermansia (Akk) colonization. We show that Akkermansia species have multiple cps loci, with cps1 being the most conserved across species. We find that cps1 is regulated by phase variation via invertible

papers.title: Strain-Specific Lactic Acid Bacteria Fermentation of Kiwifruit Juice: From Flavor Optimization to In Vitro Gut Microbiota Modulation.
papers.abstract: The deep processing of kiwifruit remains an urgent challenge. In this work, we evaluated the effects of seven lactic acid bacteria (LAB) strains on the overall quality of the kiwifruit juice. All strains exhibited robust growth, and LAB fermentation markedly improved the flavor profile of the juice, with the M29 group best preserving the characteristic kiwi fruit aroma. Sensory analysis further revealed that M29 and LB22 particularly enhanced the fruity sensory notes. In vitro fecal fermentation
papers.journal: Journal of agricultural and food chemistry

papers.title: Study on the Alleviating Effect of Tropical Psidium guajava Basal Postbiotics on Hyperuricemia.
papers.abstract: Hyperuricemia (HUA) is a growing global health concern with a younger onset trend. Using a high-purine diet-induced HUA mouse model, this study evaluated kidney, colon, and gut microbiota damage and investigated the effects of Psidium guajava basal postbiotics (PGP). PGP reduced xanthine oxidase activity, modulated purine metabolism, and increased beneficial gut bacteria (Akkermansia muciniphila) and short-chain fatty acids (acetate, butyrate, propionate). papers.journal: Journal of agricultural and food chemistry

papers.title: Early supplemental feeding improves post-weaning growth restriction in lambs via the gastrointestinal-metabolic axis.
papers.abstract: The weaning period often induces growth restriction in lambs due to gastrointestinal immaturity and dietary transition. Although early supplemental feeding (ESF) shows promise, its systemic mechanisms via the rumen microbiota-gut-metabolism axis remain incompletely understood. Sixty Tan lambs were assigned to a control group (suckling only), Trial Group I (free-access ESF from 10 days), and Trial Group II (free-access + forced isolation from 15 days). Growth performance, rumen fermentation, inte

papers.title: Multi-omics insights into the physiological mechanisms of bile acid accumulation in the gallbladder in brumation-like snakes.
papers.abstract: Hibernation/brumation represents an important physiological adaptation for animals to cope with seasonal environmental changes. Field observations suggested increased gallbladder weight in the Five-pacer viper (Deinagkistrodon acutus) during brumation, and our quantitative measurements confirmed this increase together with bile acid accumulation. By integrating a multi-omic approach, this study elucidates the regulatory mechanisms of bile acid accumulation in the gallbladder during brumation. Re
papers.journal: Comparative biochemistry and physiology. Part D, Genomics & proteomics

papers.title: A multi-organ metabolomics atlas reveals molecular dysregulations in Alzheimer's disease mouse models.
papers.abstract: The etiology of Alzheimer's disease (AD) remains unclear but is likely driven by gene-environment interactions. We present a multi-organ untargeted metabolomics atlas (n = 2,271) paired with metagenomics data (n = 666) from two AD transgenic mouse models (3xTg and 5xFAD) under colonized and germ-free conditions. Systems-level analyses revealed clusters of dysregulated molecules across tissues, including carnitines, bile acids, B vitamins, neurotransmitters, and N-acyl lipids. Metabolic shifts we

papers.title: Ligilactobacillus murinus modulates gut microbiota-derived 5-hydroxyindole to inhibit pseudorabies virus infection via activation of the aryl hydrocarbon receptor.
papers.abstract: Pseudorabies virus (PRV) infects a wide range of mammals and poses a potential threat to human health. The stimulator of interferon genes (STING) is crucial for innate immunity against DNA viruses and intestinal homeostasis; however, its influence on gut microbiota-mediated antiviral immunity is unclear. papers.title: Polysaccharide PSLP-1 from Polygonatum sibiricum Stems and Leaves Alleviates Depressive-like Behaviors and Modulates Gut Microbiota and Tryptophan Metabolism along the Gut-Brain Axis.
papers.abstract: The stems and leaves of Polygonatum sibiricum Red. are underused food byproducts whose polysaccharides and bioactivities remain poorly characterized. In this study, a novel homogeneous polysaccharide, PSLP-1 (4.287 kDa), was isolated and identified as a fructan-rich fraction with a →1)-β-d-Fruf-(2→ backbone and C6 side-chain substitution. papers.journal: Journal of agricultural and food chemistry

papers.title: Aromatic lactic acid production by Bifidobacterium longum subsp. infantis is determined by cell density, substrate availability, and pH in vitro.
papers.abstract: Bifidobacterium longum subsp. infantis (B. infantis) is an important colonizer of the infant gut. This species is known to produce a variety of health-beneficial metabolites, including aromatic lactic acids (ALAs), supporting early-life immune development. However, the regulation of ALA production in B. infantis remains poorly understood. In this study, we investigated how environmental factors, such as substrate availability and pH, affect B. infantis DSM20088 growth and ALA production in vitro

papers.title: The gut microbiota directs vitamin A flux to regulate intestinal T cell development.
papers.abstract: The intestinal microbiota shapes adaptive immunity, but the mechanisms remain incompletely defined. Here, we show that the microbiota initiates movement of retinoids-dietary vitamin A derivatives including retinol and retinoic acid-through a sequential pathway from epithelial cells to myeloid cells and ultimately to T cells in the mesenteric lymph nodes (mLNs). papers.title: Lactobacillus plantarum ameliorates high-fat-diet-induced dyslipidemia via cholesterol metabolism and gut microbiota modulation.
papers.abstract: Dyslipidemia, a typical metabolic disorder, is predominantly characterized by elevated levels of total cholesterol (TC) and/or triglycerides (TG), with disruption of cholesterol homeostasis serving as a significant contributing factor. This study demonstrated that Lactobacillus plantarum DY6 alleviated high-fat diet-induced dyslipidemia in mice by improving lipid profiles and reducing hepatic injury. Mechanistically, L. plantarum DY6 reduced the endogenous cholesterol synthesis by downregulating

papers.title: The Microbiota-Gut-Brain Axis: A New Frontier in Precision Neuropsychopharmacology.
papers.abstract: The gut microbiota has emerged as a relevant modulator of brain function, behavior, and treatment response through the microbiota-gut-brain axis. This bidirectional system may influence the pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics of psychotropic drugs, offering new perspectives for neuropsychopharmacology and precision psychiatry. papers.title: Efficacy of Adjunct PRObiotics as Compared to the Standard Care in Moderate Unipolar Depression Among Geriatric Patients: A Randomized Double-Blind Placebo-Controlled Pilot Multi-Center Trial (PRODG).
papers.abstract: To evaluate the efficacy of adjunct probiotic supplementation (Lactobacillus helveticus and Bifidobacterium longum) alongside standard care compared to placebo in older adults with moderate unipolar depression.
papers.journal: Journal of the American Geriatrics Society

papers.title: Probiotic Lactobacillus Reuteri KUB-AC5 Provides Cardioprotection via Mitigating Cardiac Mitochondrial Dysfunction in Obese Rats.
papers.abstract: Cardiac mitochondrial dysfunction is a crucial mechanism underlying obesity-induced cardiovascular diseases. A close link between obesity and gut microbiota has been revealed, and the benefits of gut microbiota modulation by probiotics have been widely identified. Although the probiotic Lactobacillus reuteri KUB-AC5 exerted anti-inflammatory activity and enhanced the activity of beneficial microbes, the effects of this probiotic on mitochondria in the obese heart have never been investigated. papers.title: Bifidobacterium lactis XLTG11 Reduces Eczema and Infections in Infants: A Randomized Trial.
papers.abstract: Early-life gut microbiota profoundly influences immune system maturation and disease susceptibility. Perturbations in microbial development have been linked to rising rates of allergic and infectious diseases in children. Probiotic interventions offer a promising strategy to restore microbial-immune homeostasis; however, evidence from rigorously designed, strain-specific randomized trials integrating clinical and microbiome outcomes remains limited.
papers.journal: QJM : monthly journal of the Association of Physicians

papers.title: Lacto-N-biose Improves Acute Colitis in Mice by Modulating Gut Microbiota and Metabolites.
papers.abstract: Lacto-N-biose (LNB) as the core structure among human milk oligosaccharides remained insufficiently characterized in terms of the potential to ameliorate colitis. This study investigated effects of LNB in colitis by using dextran sulfate sodium salt to induce mouse model. Results demonstrated that LNB significantly ameliorated the increase in disease activity index, suppressed immune factors TNF-α, F4/80, IL-6, and IFN-γ, increased factors IL-4 of colitis mice, and the high-dose group exhibited
papers.journal: Journal of agricultural and food chemistry

papers.title: The Prebiotic 2'-Fucosyllactose Ameliorates Colitis via an Akkermansia muciniphila-Mediated Prebiotic-Microbiota-Metabolite Axis.
papers.abstract: Recent studies indicate that 2'-fucosyllactose (2'-FL), a human milk oligosaccharide, alleviates inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) by modulating the gut microbiota, yet the underlying mechanisms and key functional bacteria involved remain unclear. This study employed fecal microbiota transplantation and an antibiotic-treated mouse model to investigate the role of 2'-FL-mediated gut microbiota in lipopolysaccharide-induced colitis, with a focus on Akkermansia muciniphila (A. muciniphila). Results
papers.journal: Journal of agricultural and food chemistry

papers.title: Gut microbiota, brown adipose tissue whitening, and obesity: nutritional modulators and metabolic crosstalk.
papers.abstract: Obesity reflects a chronic imbalance between energy intake and expenditure, accompanied by metabolic inflammation and ectopic lipid deposition. Accumulating evidence indicates that gut microbiota dysbiosis reshapes host nutrient handling and endocrine-immune signaling, with downstream consequences for thermogenic adipose tissues. papers.title: The microbiota-gut-brain axis: novel mechanisms and therapeutic frontiers in obesity and type 2 diabetes.
papers.abstract: The microbiota-gut-brain axis (MGBA) is a bidirectional network linking gut microbes to the CNS. This review elucidates how microbial messengers-short-chain fatty acids, bile acids, neuroactive metabolites, and extracellular vesicles-drive obesity and type 2 diabetes via neural, endocrine, and immune pathways. papers.title: Lactobacillus reuteri targets FXR-dependent bile acid homeostasis to ameliorate alcoholic liver disease.
papers.abstract: Alcoholic liver disease (ALD) is characterized by gut dysbiosis, yet the functional contribution of specific commensals remains largely unexplored. Here, we identify a significant reduction of Lactobacillus reuteri in both ALD patients and mouse models compared with their respective healthy controls. papers.title: The Regulatory Mechanisms of Indole-3-Carboxaldehyde in Immune Cells and Its Role in Autoimmune Diseases.
papers.abstract: Immune cells play a central role in the pathogenesis and progression of autoimmune diseases. Recent evidence indicates that metabolites produced by the gut microbiota are essential for maintaining immune cell homeostasis. Indole-3-carboxaldehyde (IAld), a key gut microbial metabolite, has garnered considerable attention due to its immunomodulatory properties in autoimmune diseases. In multiple sclerosis, IAld alleviates inflammation through modulation of mast cells and astrocytes; in rheumatoid

papers.title: Reduction of N-dimethylnitrosamine by Lactiplantibacillus pentosus R3 during simulated gastrointestinal digestion of fermented sausages.
papers.abstract: This study investigated the effect of Lactiplantibacillus pentosus R3 in reducing N-dimethylnitrosamine (NDMA) content during simulated gastrointestinal digestion of fermented sausages. The results showed that the content of NDMA in the fermented sausages increased during digestion (P < 0.05). Compared with the control, the NDMA content in the inoculated sausage was significantly lower (P < 0.05). papers.title: Lactobacillus johnsonii DM2420 Alleviates Dyslipidemia, Remodels Gut Microbiota, and Modulates the Intestinal CD36/SREBP1 Signaling Axis.
papers.abstract: Dyslipidemia is a core pathological manifestation of lipid metabolism disorders, and dysbiosis of gut microbiota plays a key role in its onset and progression. Building upon our previous findings on the anti-inflammatory and gut microbiota-modulating effects of Lactobacillus johnsonii DM2420, this study further investigated the regulatory effects and underlying mechanisms of this strain in high-fat diet (HFD)-induced dyslipidemia mice. In vitro, L. johnsonii DM2420 significantly inhibited the ac

papers.title: Integrative analysis of the gut-liver-heart axis in coronary artery disease: From chronic metabolic dysbiosis to acute cardiogenic shock.
papers.abstract: The complex interplay between chronic metabolic disorders and acute cardiovascular decompensation represents a critical frontier in cardiology. This integrative research program conducted at Chongqing Medical University systematically examines the gut-liver-heart axis across the cardiovascular disease spectrum through three interconnected studies. First, a case-control investigation of coronary artery disease (CAD) patients with and without nonalcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD) revealed disti

papers.title: Pro/synbiotics reduce systemic inflammation and improve gut health in Kenyan infants: An open label, randomized, 4-arm, phase II trial.
papers.abstract: Environmental enteric dysfunction, universal in young children exposed to poor sanitation and hygiene, impairs growth and development through malabsorption and as a driver of chronic systemic inflammation (CSI). In an open-label, randomized, four-arm, phase II clinical trial, infants with birthweight ≥2000 g in Homa Bay County, western Kenya receive live, multi-strain Bifidobacterium spp. and Lactobacillaceae pro/synbiotics from 0 to 6 months. papers.title: The bacteroidal metabolite O-LysoPE facilitates hepatocyte-mediated immunosuppression in autoimmune hepatitis.
papers.abstract: Autoimmune hepatitis (AIH) is a chronic inflammatory liver disease typically managed with broad-spectrum immunosuppressants that carry significant systemic side effects and often provide incomplete efficacy. While gut-microbiota-derived metabolites are known to influence AIH progression, the specific microbial drivers that maintain hepatic immune homeostasis remain poorly defined. papers.title: Gut microbiome composition and functional potential associate with incident type 2 diabetes in 4,685 adults from a Swedish prospective cohort.
papers.abstract: Cross-sectional studies link gut microbiome alterations to type 2 diabetes (T2D), but prospective evidence remains limited. We aim to identify taxonomic and functional features associated with future T2D risk. We analyze shotgun metagenomic data from 4,685 participants (mean age, 73.9 years; 49.0% women) in the Swedish SIMPLER cohort, followed for a median 5.3 years, during which 383 developed T2D. Six species are associated with increased T2D risk: Desulfovibrio piger, Alistipes communis, Alist

papers.title: Healthful plant-based diet, gut enterotype, and cognition in a rural Chinese elderly cohort: A longitudinal multi-omics study.
papers.abstract: The gut microbiome may shape how diet influences cognitive aging, but population-based evidence remains limited. In 784 older adults living in rural China (70-98 years old) with fecal metagenomics and structured dietary assessment, a modified healthful plant-based diet index (mHPDI) is associated with distinct gut microbial structure and taxonomic shifts (15 species, 17 genera). Among participants with repeated cognitive measurements, higher mHPDI is associated with better global cognition, with

papers.title: The nutrigenomic-epigenetic axis in cancer: from dietary bioactives to precision oncology.
papers.abstract: Cancer development and progression are increasingly understood to arise from dynamic interactions between genetic alterations and environmentally driven epigenetic regulation, with dietary exposures representing one of the most biologically influential and modifiable factors. Given the expanding evidence linking nutrition to epigenomic remodeling, a comprehensive synthesis of how specific dietary components influence cancer-relevant epigenetic mechanisms is needed to clarify their preventive and

papers.title: Astragalus polysaccharides ameliorate perinatal metabolic syndrome in sows via enhancing butyrate-producing bacteria.
papers.abstract: Astragalus polysaccharide (APS), a bioactive phytomacromolecule from Astragalus membranaceus, exhibits anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and immunomodulatory activities. Given mammals' lack of endogenous glycosidases for APS catabolism, this study hypothesizes its bioactivity stems from gut microbial interactions, investigating APS-mediated microbiome remodeling and therapeutic effects on perinatal metabolic syndrome (PeriMS) in sows. papers.title: Gallic acid alleviates colitis by restoring intestinal barrier function and enriching butyrate-producing bacteria.
papers.abstract: Ulcerative colitis (UC), a chronic inflammatory bowel disease, exhibits increasing global prevalence. However, there remains a paucity of evidence on whether gallic acid (GA) can be used to mitigate acute and chronic colitis. In this study, the underlying mechanism of GA in alleviating UC was thoroughly investigated. Our results showed that GA protected mice from acute and chronic colitis, evidenced by relieved pathological symptoms of UC, strengthened the tight junction, and repaired the damage

papers.title: Ulvan acts as a prebiotic polysaccharide enhancing the production of indoles and short-chain fatty acids in vitro: a metabolomics study.
papers.abstract: This study investigated the fermentability and prebiotic potential of ulvan, a sulfated polysaccharide from Ulva lactuca L., using an in vitro human colonic fermentation model. Targeted metabolomics quantified SCFA and tryptophan-derived metabolites to assess saccharolytic activity and fiber-driven modulation of amino acid metabolism. Ulvan supplementation significantly stimulated SCFA production, particularly acetic acid, achieving levels comparable to the well-established prebiotic inulin. In

papers.title: Microbial metabolites at the front line: Orchestrating gastrointestinal and systemic barrier immunity across the lifespan.
papers.abstract: Microbiota-derived metabolites are central mediators between commensal microbes and host immune system at mucosal barrier surfaces. Insights from mouse models have revealed precise molecular mechanisms by which numerous metabolites, including short-chain fatty acids, tryptophan catabolites and bile acid derivatives, regulate epithelial integrity, innate immune tone, and adaptive immunity and tolerance. papers.title: The hawthorn (Crataegus pinnatifida) procyanidin extract attenuates nonalcoholic fatty liver disease in mice via remodeling the bile acid profile driven by gut microbiota and regulating the FXR pathway.
papers.abstract: Hawthorn procyanidin extract (HPC) is one of natural plant-derived polyphenols with lipid-lowering and liver-protective properties, while its therapeutic mechanisms against nonalcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD) require further clarification. A high-fat diet (HFD)-induced NAFLD mouse model and oleic acid (OA)-induced HepG2 cells were utilized to conduct this study. We first found that HPC intervention ameliorated lipid accumulation in HepG2 cells, which was confirmed to depend on FXR signaling

papers.title: Multi-omics profiling and bile-acid exposure assays implicate a gut microbiome-parasite axis linked to persistent Entamoeba histolytica carriage.
papers.abstract: Asymptomatic Entamoeba histolytica (Eh) carriage is a major transmission reservoir, yet how the gut ecosystem-particularly microbiota-derived metabolites such as secondary bile acids-supports persistent colonization remains unclear. We investigated whether gut microbiome-metabolite features are associated with Eh carriage and could influence parasite phenotypes METHODS: We integrated shotgun metagenomics from a prospectively screened outpatient cohort (n=36) with functional in vitro assays. papers.title: Bile Salts and Bacterial Bile Salt Hydrolase Activity Influence Escherichia coli Colonization and FXR Signaling in Colorectal Cancer Organoids.
papers.abstract: Bile salts are key regulators of intestinal homeostasis and host-microbe interactions in the intestine. While bile salt hydrolase (BSH) activity is known to modulate host metabolism, the direct interplay between bile salt signaling, microbial BSH activity, bile acid signaling, and epithelial responses in colorectal cancer remains poorly understood. papers.title: The gut microbiome in preterm infants: development, dysbiosis, and disease implications.
papers.abstract: SUMMARYPreterm infants face a unique trajectory of gut microbiome assembly, shaped by the convergence of physiological immaturity, necessary clinical interventions, and the neonatal intensive care unit environment. This dysbiotic ecosystem-characterized by low diversity, depletion of beneficial commensals such as Bifidobacterium, and expansion of pathobionts, including Enterobacteriaceae-is increasingly recognized as a central mediator of neonatal morbidity. papers.title: Probiotic, synbiotic effects on the gut-liver axis: omics-enabled mechanisms and therapeutic windows.
papers.abstract: The gut-liver axis is a two-way communication network where gut microbes and their metabolites affect liver function, while the liver regulates the intestinal environment through bile acids, immune factors, and antimicrobial substances. Disruption of this balance contributes to various liver diseases, including nonalcoholic fatty liver disease, alcohol-associated liver disease, cirrhosis, and liver cancer. papers.title: Tryptophan metabolism as a key integrator within the gut-lung-brain axis: Mechanistic insights and nutritional therapeutic strategies for inflammatory and neuropsychiatric disorders.
papers.abstract: The gut-lung-brain (GLB) axis is a multidirectional communication network linking the gastrointestinal tract, respiratory system, and central nervous system (CNS) through neural, endocrine, and immune pathways. Emerging evidence suggests that tryptophan (Trp) metabolism serves as a key integrating node within this axis, modulating host-microbe interactions involved in systemic homeostasis. papers.title: NIR-II Neuromodulation Combined with Metabolite-Mediated Immunoregulation for Accelerated Deeply Located Nerve Repair.
papers.abstract: Deep peripheral nerve regeneration is hindered by inflammatory infection, neurotrophic factor deficiency, and slow axonal growth kinetics. Although multifunctional nerve guidance conduits (NGCs) have been developed, achieving spatiotemporally precise neuromodulation within deeply located neural tissues remains a significant challenge. papers.title: Lonicera japonica polysaccharide restores mucosal integrity in ulcerative colitis by increasing microbiota-derived spermidine.
papers.abstract: WLJP-025p, a homogeneous polysaccharide from Lonicera japonica Thunb., alleviates experimental ulcerative colitis in mice by reshaping the gut microbiota and restoring intestinal spermidine. Microbial fermentation, microbiota depletion, and microbiota transplantation support a microbiota-dependent mechanism. papers.title: Galactose-functionalized engineered outer membrane vesicles for targeted delivery of enterocin Gr17 ameliorate Salmonella Typhimurium-induced colitis.
papers.abstract: Bacteriocins, antimicrobial peptides from lactic acid bacteria (LAB), represent promising agents against Salmonella Typhimurium-induced colitis. However, their proteinaceous nature leads to poor bioavailability and insufficient concentrations at infection sites, which significantly limits their application potential. Enterocin Gr17 exhibits potent antibacterial activity against S. typhimurium and effectively ameliorates pathogen-induced intestinal barrier damage. Herein, we innovatively develope

papers.title: Gum arabic-coated nano-nutriosome for gastrointestinal delivery of cyanidin-3-O-glucoside and application in yogurt as a real food system.
papers.abstract: Cyanidin-3-O-glucoside (C3G), a promising flavonoid with diverse biological properties, faces challenges in functional food application due to instability under various conditions. Therefore, this study aimed to develop a novel gum Arabic-coated nano-nutriosome (GA-C3G-NU) for promoting gastro-intestinal (GI) delivery of C3G and fortification of set-type yogurt (Ygr). papers.title: Alleviating effects of 2'-fucosyllactose combined with Bifidobacterium longum BB536 on loperamide-induced constipation and depressive-like behavior.
papers.abstract: Constipation as a public health issue impairs human health and quality of life, while current strategies against constipation often lead to adverse effects. This study evaluated the effects and underlying mechanism of 2'-fucosyllactose (2'-FL) and Bifidobacterium longum BB536 (BB536) on relieving loperamide-induced constipation and depressive-like behavior. 2'-FL + BB536 can significantly elevate fecal water content, shorten defecation time and enhance the intestinal transit rate in constipated

papers.title: 6'-Sialyllactose Combined with Bifidobacterium animalis subsp. lactis BB12 Relieves Constipation and Depression-like Behaviors Induced by Loperamide in Mice by Regulating the Gut-Brain Axis.
papers.abstract: Constipation is a common gastrointestinal disorder, which impairs health and quality of life. This study evaluated the ameliorating effects and underlying mechanism of 6'-sialyllactose (6'-SL) and Bifidobacterium animalis subsp. lactis BB12 (BB12) on loperamide-induced constipation and associated depression-like behaviors. 6'-SL combined with BB12 increased the fecal water content, fecal pellet number, and gastrointestinal transit rate while reducing the time of first black feces. 6'-SL and BB12
papers.journal: Journal of agricultural and food chemistry

papers.title: Microplastics-Induced Gut Microbiota Dysbiosis Accelerates Alzheimer's-Like Pathology and Cognitive Decline via the Gut-Brain Axis.
papers.abstract: Alzheimer's disease (AD) is incurable and increasingly attributed to gene-environment interactions. Microplastics (MPs) are omnipresent in the human food chain, yet their impact on neurodegeneration is largely unknown. Here we show that chronic oral exposure to 2-µm amine-modified polystyrene microparticles accelerates cognitive decline, amplifies Aβ deposition, gliosis, and synaptic loss, and cripples autophagic flux in 5XFAD mice through the gut-brain axis. papers.title: Bifidobacteria infantis and human milk oligosaccharides have independent and synergistic effects on immune response and amino acid metabolism in germ-free mouse models.
papers.abstract: Early-life microbial colonization is essential for gut and immune development. Human milk oligosaccharides (HMOs) support the growth of Bifidobacterium infantis (BI). Here, we studied the individual and combined effects of BI and HMOs on the immune and colon transcriptomes and on serum and cecal metabolome. Germ-free mice were randomly assigned to four groups (10-14/group: HMO, BI, BI + HMO, and control [no HMO or BI]). HMO and BI + HMO groups received 5 mg/day each of 2'-fucosyllactose, lacto-N

papers.title: Hypocholesterolemic agents and the gut microbiota: a review of interactions and modulatory activity.
papers.abstract: Hypercholesterolemia is a major risk factor that contributes to the development and/or aggravation of cardiovascular diseases. High cholesterol levels are frequently managed with hypocholesterolemic agents, either synthetic or natural. Beyond their cholesterol-lowering effects, these compounds can also affect the host's gut microbiota. papers.title: In vitro modeling of inflammatory bowel diseases using a newly developed immunocompetent colon epithelial monolayer co-culture model.
papers.abstract: Clinical treatment of inflammatory bowel diseases (IBD) remains challenging due to the complex interplay between the epithelial barrier, immune system, and gut microbiota. While in vitro models are pivotal for studying barrier dysfunction, developing a standardized and functionally relevant system for IBD remains challenging. To overcome this, we established an immunocompetent murine colon epithelium monolayer to model IBD-like conditions. Colons from wild-type mice were digested into single cel

papers.title: UPLC-Q-TOF-MS/MS-based dynamic metabolite profile analysis of Huangkui capsule in serum, urine, and feces from normal and pseudo-germ-free rats.
papers.abstract: Huangkui capsule (HKC), a proprietary traditional Chinese medicine, has been used for decades to clinically treat chronic kidney diseases. Gut microbiota may mediate its metabolism, yet inadequate research on HKC's in vivo metabolism hinders understanding of its pharmacodynamic basis. In this study, normal and pseudo-germ-free rats were orally administered HKC, with serum, urine, and fecal samples collected at serial time points. All samples were analyzed by UPLC-Q-TOF-MS/MS. papers.title: Food-grade TiO2 impairs intestinal mucus barrier via disrupting the gut microbiota-ILA-mucin sulfation axis: novel insights and dietary intervention strategies.
papers.abstract: The use of titanium dioxide (TiO2) as a food additive has persistently elicited concerns about its potential toxicity, primarily attributed to the significant presence of nanoparticles. Studies have recently demonstrated that nano- or micro-sized food-grade TiO2 (fg-TiO2) particles can disrupt gut microbial balance and weaken the intestinal barrier; however, the underlying mechanisms remain poorly understood. papers.title: Gut microbiota reshaped by exercise improved glycolipid metabolism in obese mice via increasing the production of medium and long chain fatty acids: a multi-omics study.
papers.abstract: Exercise is effective in combating obesity and regulating the composition of the gut microbiota. However, the molecular mechanism by which exercise alters gut microbiota and its metabolites to exert weight loss has not been fully elucidated. In this study, the mechanism of gut microbiota and microbial metabolites reshaped by exercise in weight loss were investigated by macrogenomic sequencing, metabolomics analysis and fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT). papers.title: Identification and characterization of polysaccharide from Paeoniae Radix Rubra, and its mechanism in alleviating anti-tuberculosis drug-induced liver injury based on integrating gut microbiota and non-targeted metabolomics.
papers.abstract: A novel polysaccharide, designated PRRP2, was extracted from the dried root of Paeoniae Radix Rubra. Structural analysis suggested that the PRRP2 backbone was predominantly composed of →4)-Glcp-(1→ and →6)-Glcp-(1→ glycosidic linkages, with a trace amount of →2)-Xylp-(1→ embedded within the backbone. papers.journal: Journal of pharmaceutical and biomedical analysis

papers.title: Trimethylamine N-oxide in exercise physiology: a gut microbiota-derived signal linking metabolic stress, redox balance and cardiometabolic health.
papers.abstract: Trimethylamine N-oxide (TMAO) is a gut microbiota-derived metabolite traditionally associated with cardiovascular and metabolic disease risk; however, emerging evidence from exercise physiology and metabolomics suggests a more complex and context-dependent biological role. This narrative review synthesizes findings from human observational studies, exercise interventions, supplementation trials, and animal models to examine TMAO metabolism, physiological functions, and its relationship with phys

papers.title: Functional and clinical significance of Fusicatenibacter spp.-Parasutterella spp. co-occurrence in gut dysbiosis-associated diseases: An ecological unit perspective.
papers.abstract: The co-occurrence of Fusicatenibacter spp. (Fus), and Parasutterella spp. (Par) in the human gut is widely reported. However, no studies have systematically validated these genera as a possible functional ecological unit or characterized their coordinated dynamics, metabolic coupling mechanisms, or relevance to host health and disease. papers.title: Effects of probiotics on alcohol-conditioned behaviors and hippocampal morphology in male rats.
papers.abstract: Alcohol Use Disorder (AUD) is a complex condition marked by physical and psychological dependence, often accompanied by lasting changes in brain regions such as the hippocampus that contribute to memory and cognitive impairments. Emerging evidence highlights the role of gut microbiota dysbiosis in AUD and suggests a link between the gut-brain axis, neuroinflammation, and behavioral alterations. Probiotics have been proposed as potential modulators of microbial balance and inflammatory responses,

papers.title: Targeting the gut-brain axis: microbial interventions for neurological disorders.
papers.abstract: The pathogenesis of neurological disorders involves complex interactions among genetic, environmental, immunological, and metabolic factors. Characterized by high disability rates and prolonged disease courses, these conditions impose a significant burden on patients and society. As a dynamic and modifiable component of the internal environment, gut microbiota plays a central role in the onset and progression of neurological disorders through the "gut-brain axis."
